title: ‘Virginia Woolf’s Night & Day’ Review: Haley Bennett Is Starry-Eyed in a Literary Adaptation With Much Heart and a Heavy Hand
content:
Lily Allen, Jack Whitehall and Timothy Spall also star in Tina Gharavi's SXSW London opener, which refashions Woolf's diffuse, ruminative feminist novel into a straightforwardly inspirational tale.

ByGuy Lodge

Film Critic

Virginia Woolf herself was not the greatest admirer of her 1919 novel “Night and Day,” a complex and somewhat elusive work that wove a pensive reflection on women’s suffrage through a quasi-Shakespearean rotation of misbegotten and rearranged courtships — in a style far removed from the angular modernism of her later works. It remains perhaps the most underexposed of her books, and though it’s easy to imagine the period romantic comedy that Merchant-Ivory-style filmmakers might have made of it, it’s taken until now for anyone to attempt an adaptation. ThoughTina Gharavi‘s film stresses its allegiance to the text with the title “Virginia Woolf’s Night & Day,” it’s actually quite a departure: Playing down the novel’s tangled relationships in favor of a straightforwardly empowering celebration of female agency and education, it trades some of the author’s elegance and nuance for a more crowdpleasing message.

Whether it finds many crowds to please remains to be seen. London-set and broadly accessible, “Virginia Woolf’s Night and Day” was a fitting opener for the second edition of the SXSW London multimedia fest, a few weeks ahead of its U.K. theatrical bow. But given its relatively low-profile source material and a solid cast of known names who nonetheless aren’t major big-screen draws, the film might fare better on streaming platforms internationally. For Iranian-born filmmaker Gharavi, who landed a BAFTA nomination for her punchy 2013 debut feature “I Am Nasrine,” this handsomely dressed and mounted production proves she can handle the demands of British heritage cinema, though it’s a less interesting direction for her.

An American who can seem amorphously international when required in such projects as “Cyrano” and “Widow Clicquot,”Haley Bennettis a vivacious and likable anchor for the film around her. She sports a convincing cut-glass accent as Katherine (or Kit, when the mood takes her), a spirited and intellectually curious young woman in Edwardian London with a particular passion for astronomy — one of many fields of study then barred to women, even relatively well-to-do ones like Katherine. She must disguise herself as a man to attend lectures at the Royal Astronomical Society, while her dreams of continuing her personal research at Cambridge face a patriarchal wall of opposition.

Her stuffy father (Timothy Spall) would prefer she find herself a suitable husband; she eventually accepts the proposal of her childhood friend William (comedian Jack Whitehall, easily adapting his signature posh-bozo persona to the period), a foppish and untalented poet, to get everyone off her back. Her cousin Cyril (Misia Butler), her closest male ally, is aghast at her pragmatism in this regard; in a major change from the novel, where the character unapologetically fathered children out of wedlock, here he’s a marginalized gay man, unwilling to live a lie to cut an easier path through the world. Naturally, no sooner has Katherine entered a loveless engagement than she strikes sparks with Ralph (Elyas M’Barek), a literary editor commissioned by her father to tame the unwieldy manuscript of her would-be writer mother (Jennifer Saunders) — an ostensibly kind but ultimately controlling male gesture.

Though it’s at the core of the novel, Katherine’s relationship with Ralph never comes into focus in Justine Waddell’s adaptation, as every male character bar Cyril is given pointedly short shrift in the film. More screen time is given over to her burgeoning friendship with firebrand suffragette Mary, played by singerLily Allenin a deliberately anachronistic performance — her forthright speech and manner beamed in directly from the 21st century. The two women bond more closely here than they do in the novel, where their individualist and more community-minded stances were subtly contrasted; the film prefers a more robustly unified representation of female solidarity, driven home by dialogue that comes close to speechifying at several points. (On at least one occasion, when an incensed Katherine gives a sexist university selection panel what for, this streamlined progressive rhetoric is quite satisfying.)

Still, sometimes Bennett’s lively, headstrong performance feels like it’s swimming not just against outdated social currents, but the film’s own staidness. As much as Gharavi tries to energize proceedings with a bobbing handheld camera and an electro-tinged score that, in the closing credits, finally bubbles over into ethereal, Ellie Goulding-style pop, “Virginia Woolf’s Night & Day” can feel talky and stiffly didactic, however sincere in its convictions. Well-meaning but ultimately familiar in both message and delivery, the film speaks much of the bolder future ahead, but the filmmaking does little to disrupt the status quo.
title_zh: 《維吉尼亞·伍爾夫的夜與日》(Virginia Woolf’s Night & Day) 評論：海莉·班奈特(Haley Bennett) 在這部充滿情感且手法沉重的文學改編作品中閃耀著星光。
content_zh:
莉莉·艾倫(Lily Allen)、傑克·懷特霍爾(Jack Whitehall)和提摩西·斯波(Timothy Spall)也參演了蒂娜·加哈維(Tina Gharavi)的SXSW倫敦開幕片，該片將伍爾夫(Virginia Woolf)模糊而沉思的女性主義小說重新塑造成一個直截了當的鼓舞人心的故事。

作者：蓋·洛奇(Guy Lodge)

電影評論家

維吉尼亞·伍爾夫(Virginia Woolf)本人並不是她1919年小說《夜與日》(Night and Day)的最大讚賞者，這是一部複雜且有些難以捉摸的作品，通過一系列錯綜複雜和重新排列的戀愛關係，編織出對女性選舉權的深思反省——其風格與她後期作品的尖銳現代主義相去甚遠。這本書或許是她作品中曝光率最低的一本，儘管很容易想像像梅丘爾-艾佛瑞(Merchant-Ivory)風格的電影製作人會如何改編它，但直到現在才有人嘗試進行改編。雖然蒂娜·加哈維的電影強調其對文本的忠誠，並以《維吉尼亞·伍爾夫的夜與日》(Virginia Woolf’s Night & Day)為名，但實際上這是一個相當大的偏離：它淡化了小說中錯綜複雜的關係，轉而強調女性主體性和教育的直截了當的賦權慶祝，為了更迎合觀眾的訊息而犧牲了一些作者的優雅和細膩。

它是否能找到許多觀眾來取悅仍有待觀察。以倫敦為背景且廣泛可及的《維吉尼亞·伍爾夫的夜與日》是SXSW倫敦多媒體節第二屆的合適開幕片，距離其在英國的影院首映還有幾週。但考慮到其相對低調的來源材料以及一個固有的知名演員陣容，儘管他們並不是主要的銀幕吸引力，這部電影在國際流媒體平台上的表現可能會更好。對於伊朗出生的導演加哈維來說，她因其2013年充滿衝擊力的首部長片《我是納斯琳》(I Am Nasrine)而獲得BAFTA提名，這部精美製作的作品證明了她能夠應對英國傳承電影的要求，儘管這對她來說是一個不太有趣的方向。

海莉·班奈特(Haley Bennett)在《西拉諾》(Cyrano)和《寡婦克里科(Widow Clicquot)》等項目中，當需要時能顯得模糊國際化，是這部電影的活潑且令人喜愛的核心。她以令人信服的切割玻璃口音飾演凱瑟琳(Katherine)（或當心情好時稱為基特(Kit)），是一位在愛德華時代倫敦中充滿活力且智力好奇的年輕女性，對天文學有著特別的熱情——這是當時許多女性，包括像凱瑟琳這樣相對富裕的女性都被禁止學習的領域之一。她必須偽裝成男性才能參加皇家天文學會的講座，而她在劍橋繼續個人研究的夢想則面臨著父權的反對牆。

她那古板的父親（提摩西·斯波爾(Timothy Spall)）希望她能找到一位合適的丈夫；最終，她接受了童年好友威廉（喜劇演員傑克·懷特霍爾(Jack Whitehall)的提議，他輕鬆地將其標誌性的優雅傻瓜形象適應於這個時代），這位花花公子且無才的詩人，為了讓大家不再煩擾她。她的表親西里爾（米西亞·巴特勒(Misia Butler)）是她最親近的男性盟友，對她在這方面的務實感到震驚；與小說中的角色毫不避諱地在婚外生子相比，這裡他是一位邊緣化的同性戀者，不願意為了更容易地在世界上生活而活在謊言中。自然，凱瑟琳（Katherine）一旦進入了一段沒有愛的訂婚，便與拉爾夫（艾利亞斯·姆巴雷克(Elyas M’Barek)）擦出了火花，拉爾夫是一位由她父親委託的文學編輯，負責整理她那位想成為作家的母親（珍妮弗·桑德斯(Jennifer Saunders)）的繁瑣手稿——這是一種表面上友好但最終卻是控制的男性姿態。

儘管這是小說的核心，凱瑟琳與拉爾夫的關係在賈斯廷·瓦德爾（Justine Waddell）的改編中從未成為焦點，因為除了西里爾以外的每個男性角色在影片中都受到明顯的輕視。影片更多地給予她與激進女權主義者瑪麗的友誼時間，瑪麗由歌手莉莉·艾倫（Lily Allen）飾演，表現故意帶有時代錯位——她直率的言辭和舉止彷彿直接來自21世紀。這兩位女性在這裡的聯繫比小說中更為緊密，小說中她們的個人主義和更具社區意識的立場被微妙地對比；影片更傾向於展現女性團結的強烈統一性，這一點在幾個地方的對話中幾乎接近說教。（至少有一次，當憤怒的凱瑟琳對一個性別歧視的選拔小組發表意見時，這種簡化的進步言論相當令人滿意。）

不過，有時班奈特（Bennett）活潑而倔強的表現感覺不僅是在與過時的社會潮流作鬥爭，也是在與影片自身的沉悶作鬥爭。儘管加哈維（Gharavi）試圖用搖晃的手持攝影機和帶有電子音色的配樂來激活情節，並在片尾字幕中最終轉化為空靈的艾莉·古爾丁（Ellie Goulding）風格的流行音樂，《弗吉尼亞·伍爾夫的白天與黑夜》（Virginia Woolf’s Night & Day）有時仍會感到冗長和生硬的說教，無論其信念多麼真誠。這部影片雖然出於善意，但在信息和表達上最終仍顯得熟悉，談論著更大膽的未來，但其製作並未對現狀造成太大衝擊。
summary_zh: 莉莉·艾倫(Lily Allen)、傑克·懷特霍爾(Jack Whitehall)和提摩西·斯波(Timothy Spall)也參演了蒂娜·加哈維(Tina Gharavi)的SXSW倫敦開幕片，該片將伍爾夫(Virginia Woolf)模糊而沉思的女性主義小說重新塑造成一個直截了當的鼓舞人心的故事。
